notes: Regular -ar verb.
Acabar de + infinitive = "to have just done something."
Can mean "to finish," "to end," or "to wind up."
Frequently used in everyday speech.

examples.es: Acabé mi tarea antes de cenar.
examples.en: I finished my homework before dinner.

overview: Used to express finishing, ending, or completing an action. It is also commonly used in the phrase acabar de + infinitive to indicate that something has just happened. Because of this construction, it is one of the most useful verbs for discussing completed actions and recent events.

notes: Irregular in the preterite (anduve, anduviste, etc.).
Can mean "to walk," "to travel around," or "to be doing."
Frequently used in expressions like andar buscando (to be looking for).

examples.es: Anduvimos por la ciudad toda la tarde.
examples.en: We walked around the city all afternoon.

overview: Primarily means "to walk" or "to go around," but it is often used more broadly to describe how someone is doing, functioning, or moving about. It is common in everyday conversation and has some irregular forms.

notes: Regular -er verb.
Often followed by a noun or infinitive.
Common in educational and professional contexts.

examples.es: Aprendió a nadar cuando era niño.
examples.en: He learned to swim when he was a child.

examples.es: Queremos aprender más sobre la historia.
examples.en: We want to learn more about history.

overview: Means "to learn." It is a very common verb used when talking about acquiring knowledge, skills, languages, or information.

notes: Irregular verb.
First-person present: caigo.
Also used figuratively (caer bien = to make a good impression).
Use caerse when referring to a person falling accidentally.

examples.en: The leaf fell from the tree.
I like her / She makes a good impression on me.

overview: Means "to fall" or "to drop." It can refer to physical falling as well as figurative uses. The reflexive form caerse is commonly used when a person falls down accidentally.

english: to fall down, to fall over
notes: Irregular verb — shares the same stems as caer.
Commonly used for personal accidents and unintentional falls.
The reflexive pronoun (me, te, se, etc.) must agree with the subject.

examples.es: El niño se cayó en el parque.
examples.en: The child fell down in the park.

overview: The reflexive form of caer, used specifically when a person or thing falls down accidentally. The reflexive construction emphasises that the fall is unintentional and affects the subject directly.

notes: Regular -ar verb.
Often used when discussing music, performances, and celebrations.
Helpful as a model verb for beginning students.

examples.es: Canto en el coro de la escuela.
examples.en: I sing in the school choir.

overview: Means "to sing." It is a regular and commonly taught verb that serves as a model for many regular -ar verb conjugations.

notes: Stem-changing verb (e → ie) in many present-tense forms.
Similar in meaning to empezar.
Often followed by a + infinitive.

examples.es: La clase comienza a las ocho.

overview: Means "to begin" or "to start." It is commonly used for events, projects, activities, and actions that are getting underway.

notes: Regular -er verb.
Used for meals, food habits, and dining.
Important model verb for learning -er conjugations.

examples.en: What do you want to eat?

overview: Means "to eat." It is one of the most frequently used verbs in Spanish and serves as a model for regular -er verbs.

notes: Regular -ar verb.
Often used with stores, products, and prices.
Very common in everyday conversation.

examples.en: We are going to buy food.

overview: Means "to buy" or "to purchase." It is commonly used when discussing shopping, transactions, and obtaining goods or services.

notes: Irregular -cir verb.
First-person present: conduzco.
Commonly used for driving cars and other vehicles.

examples.en: I drive to work every day.

overview: Means "to drive," "to lead," or "to conduct." It is especially common when talking about operating vehicles, but can also refer to guiding or directing.

notes: First-person present: conozco.
Used for people, cities, and experiences.
Remember: conocer = familiarity; saber = knowledge/facts.
overview: Means "to know" people, places, or things through familiarity or personal experience. It differs from saber, which refers to factual knowledge.

notes: Irregular -ir verb.
Forms insert a y in many present and preterite forms (construyo, construyó).
Common in construction, engineering, and metaphorical contexts.

examples.en: They are building a new house.
It is important to build trust.

overview: Means "to build" or "to construct." It is used for physical structures as well as abstract projects, plans, or ideas.

notes: Stem-changing verb (o → ue) in many present-tense forms.
One of the most commonly used dual-meaning verbs.
Contar con means "to count on" or "to rely on."

examples.en: I am going to count to ten.
He told us an interesting story.
I can always count on you.

overview: Can mean either "to count" or "to tell" (a story, fact, or event). Context determines which meaning is intended.

notes: Regular -er verb with some spelling changes in certain tenses.
Frequently used with que to introduce opinions.
Can express uncertainty or confidence.

examples.en: They did not believe the news.

overview: Means "to believe" or "to think." It is commonly used for opinions, assumptions, beliefs, and trust.

notes: Irregular verb.
Appears in many idiomatic expressions.
Frequently used in daily conversation.
overview: Means "to give." It is a highly common irregular verb used in countless expressions involving giving, providing, causing, or producing.

notes: Regular -er verb.
Deber + infinitive often means "should."
Deber de + infinitive may indicate probability in some regions.
overview: Means "to owe," "should," or "must," depending on context. It is frequently used to express obligations, responsibilities, and recommendations.

notes: Highly irregular verb.
First-person present: digo.
Often used with indirect objects (decirle a alguien).
Many common expressions use decir.
overview: Means "to say" or "to tell." It is one of the most important and frequently used Spanish verbs, appearing in countless everyday conversations.

notes: Stem-changing verb (o → ue) in many present-tense forms.
Changes o → u in many preterite forms (durmió, durmieron).
Can also mean "to put to sleep" in some contexts.

examples.en: I sleep eight hours every night.

examples.es: El bebé durmió toda la noche.

overview: Means "to sleep." It is an essential verb used to describe sleeping, resting, and sleep habits. It is a stem-changing verb and one of the most commonly used -ir verbs.

notes: Regular -ar verb.
Often followed by en when specifying a place.
Common in both literal and figurative contexts.
overview: Means "to enter," "to go in," or "to come in." It is commonly used when talking about movement into places, rooms, buildings, or situations.

notes: Regular -ir verb with some spelling changes in certain tenses.
Commonly used in academic and professional settings.
Frequently appears with direct objects such as carta, correo, and mensaje.

examples.en: Can you write your name here?

overview: Means "to write." It is used for writing letters, messages, books, notes, and other forms of communication.

notes: Highly irregular verb.
Used for emotions, locations, and conditions.
Forms the present progressive: estar + gerund.
overview: Means "to be" and is used for temporary states, conditions, locations, and ongoing actions. It contrasts with ser, which is generally used for more permanent characteristics.

notes: Regular -ar verb.
Can be followed by a subject or field of study.
Very common in educational contexts.

examples.en: What do you study at the university?

overview: Means "to study." It is commonly used when discussing school, learning, preparation, and academic subjects.

notes: Often taught as an impersonal-style verb.
Me gusta literally means "it pleases me."
Requires indirect object pronouns (me, te, le, nos, os, les).
Singular and plural forms depend on what is liked.
overview: Means "to be pleasing to" and is used to express likes and dislikes. Unlike English, the thing liked is the grammatical subject, making the sentence structure unique.

notes: Highly irregular.
Essential for the present perfect and other compound tenses.
Hay is one of the most common words in Spanish.
overview: Primarily functions as an auxiliary verb used to form perfect tenses. It also appears impersonally to mean "there is" or "there are" (hay).

notes: Regular -ar verb.
Often followed by con when speaking with someone.
Can also be used for languages and topics.
overview: Means "to speak" or "to talk." It is a regular and extremely common verb that serves as a model for many -ar verb conjugations.

notes: Highly irregular.
Used in numerous expressions involving actions, weather, and time.
First-person present: hago.
overview: Means "to do" or "to make." It is one of the most versatile and frequently used verbs in Spanish.

notes: Highly irregular.
Ir a + infinitive forms the near future.
One of the most frequently used verbs in conversation.

examples.en: We are going to study tomorrow.

overview: Means "to go." It is one of the most essential verbs in Spanish, used for movement, plans, and the near future construction ir a + infinitive.

notes: Highly irregular — shares the same stems as ir.
Emphasises departure rather than destination.
Often used to say goodbye or indicate someone is leaving.
overview: The reflexive form of ir, used to emphasise leaving or departing from a place. Where ir focuses on the destination, irse focuses on the act of going away.

notes: Regular -er verb with spelling changes in some forms.
Commonly used in educational and professional contexts.
Also used figuratively for interpreting information.

examples.en: I am reading an interesting book.

overview: Means "to read." It is used for books, messages, articles, signs, and any written material.

notes: Regular -ar verb.
Llamar por teléfono = to call by phone.
Can be used to call attention or summon someone.

examples.es: Voy a llamar a mi madre.
examples.en: I am going to call my mother.

examples.es: Llama al médico si te sientes mal.
examples.en: Call the doctor if you feel unwell.

overview: Means "to call" or "to name." It is used for phone calls, calling out to someone, and naming things or people.

english: to be called, to be named
notes: Regular -ar verb in reflexive form.
¿Cómo te llamas? is one of the first questions Spanish learners encounter.
Means "to be called" or "to be named."

examples.en: Our names are Ana and Pedro.

overview: The reflexive form of llamar, used to state someone's name. It is one of the first constructions Spanish learners encounter and is essential for introductions.

english: to carry, to wear, to take
notes: Regular -ar verb.
Can refer to transporting something, wearing clothing, or the passage of time.
Context determines the exact meaning.
Common expression: llevar + time period + gerund ("to have been doing something for...").

examples.en: She is wearing a blue dress.
We have been living here for two years.

overview: Means "to carry," "to take," "to wear," or "to bring," depending on context. It is a highly versatile verb that appears frequently in everyday conversation.

notes: Regular -ar verb.
Mirar is often used where English uses "watch."
Different from ver ("to see"), which emphasizes perception rather than intentional looking.

examples.es: Miro la televisión todas las noches.

overview: Means "to look at" or "to watch." It is commonly used for observing people, objects, and events, and differs from ver ("to see"), which emphasises perception rather than intentional looking.

notes: Regular -ar verb in reflexive form.
Most frequently used in the context of looking in a mirror.
The reflexive pronoun (me, te, se, etc.) must agree with the subject.

examples.en: He looked at himself in the mirror.
I looked at myself before leaving.

examples.es: Se miraron el uno al otro.

overview: The reflexive form of mirar, meaning "to look at oneself" or "to examine oneself." It most commonly refers to looking in a mirror.

notes: Irregular verb with spelling and stem changes.
Different from escuchar ("to listen"), which implies active attention.
Frequently used when discussing sounds, conversations, and music.

examples.en: I hear music from my room.
I heard an interesting piece of news.

overview: Means "to hear." It refers to perceiving sounds and is closely related to listening and auditory understanding.

notes: Regular -ar verb with spelling changes in some forms (pagué).
Often used with bills, services, and purchases.
Common expression: pagar por ("to pay for").

examples.es: ¿Quién va a pagar por la cena?
examples.en: Who is going to pay for dinner?
They pay the rent every month.

overview: Means "to pay." It is commonly used when discussing purchases, bills, debts, and financial transactions.

notes: Stem-changing verb (e → ie) in many present forms.
Pensar en = to think about.
Pensar que = to think that.
Can also mean "to plan" when followed by an infinitive.
overview: Means "to think." It is used for opinions, plans, ideas, and mental processes. It is one of the most frequently used verbs in Spanish.

notes: Stem-changing verb (e → ie) in many present forms.
Frequently used for lost items and missed chances.
Can also mean "to waste."

examples.en: I don't want to lose this opportunity.

overview: Means "to lose" or "to miss." It can refer to losing objects, opportunities, games, or missing events and transportation.

notes: Stem-changing verb (o → ue) in many present forms.
Highly common in everyday speech.
Usually followed by an infinitive.
One of the most useful verbs for beginners.

examples.en: They were not able to enter.

overview: Means "to be able to" or "can." It is one of the most important modal verbs in Spanish and is used to express ability, possibility, and permission.

notes: Irregular verb; first-person present is pongo.
Used for placing objects, setting things up, and many idiomatic expressions.
See also ponerse for the reflexive uses (putting on clothing, becoming a state).

examples.es: Pongo el libro sobre la mesa.
examples.en: I put the book on the table.
Where do you put the keys?
They put the music on very loud.

overview: Means "to put" or "to place." It is highly versatile and appears in a wide range of everyday expressions and contexts.

notes: Irregular verb — shares the same stems as poner.
Ponerse ropa = to put on clothes.
Ponerse + adjective = to become (a temporary state).
Extremely common in daily conversation.

examples.en: Put on your coat, it's cold.

overview: The reflexive form of poner, with two key meanings: "to put on" clothing, and "to become" a temporary state or emotion. Both uses are extremely common in everyday conversation.

notes: Reflexive form of quedar.
Commonly used for staying somewhere.
Can describe resulting states or situations.
Frequently appears in everyday conversation.

examples.en: He fell asleep / ended up asleep.

overview: Means "to stay," "to remain," or sometimes "to keep." It often indicates remaining in a place or ending up in a certain condition.

notes: Stem-changing verb (e → ie) in many present forms.
Querer + infinitive = to want to do something.
Can express affection, though usually less strongly than amar.
Very common in requests and everyday conversation.

examples.en: I love you very much / I care about you very much.
What do you want to do?

overview: Means "to want" or "to love." The exact meaning depends on context, making it one of the most versatile and common Spanish verbs.

notes: Irregular verb.
Used for facts, knowledge, and skills.
Saber + infinitive = to know how to do something.
Important contrast: saber vs. conocer.

examples.en: Do you know how to swim?

overview: Means "to know" information, facts, or how to do something. It differs from conocer, which refers to familiarity with people, places, or experiences.

notes: Irregular verb; first-person present is salgo.
Can refer to leaving a location or going out socially.
Used in many everyday expressions.
Often paired with destinations or activities.

examples.es: Salgo de la oficina a las cinco.
examples.en: I leave the office at five.
We are going to go out tonight.

overview: Means "to leave," "to go out," "to come out," or "to exit." It is commonly used for departures, social outings, and movement from one place to another.

notes: Stem-changing verb (e → ie) in many present forms.
Sentir often refers to feeling something directly.
Lo siento is a very common way to say "I'm sorry."

examples.en: I feel that something has changed.

overview: Means "to feel" (an emotion or physical sensation) or "to regret." It is used when the subject directly experiences or perceives something. See also sentirse for describing how someone feels about themselves.

notes: Stem-changing verb (e → ie) — shares the same stems as sentir.
Commonly followed by adjectives describing emotional or physical states.
The reflexive pronoun (me, te, se, etc.) must agree with the subject.

examples.en: He has been feeling unwell since yesterday.

overview: The reflexive form of sentir, used to describe how someone feels physically or emotionally. It is essential for discussing health, mood, and personal experiences.

notes: Highly irregular verb.
Used for identity, origin, profession, and description.
Often contrasted with estar.
One of the most important verbs in Spanish.
overview: Means "to be" and is used for identities, characteristics, origins, occupations, relationships, and other qualities viewed as inherent or permanent. It is one of the two Spanish verbs meaning "to be," contrasted with estar.

notes: Irregular verb.
Used in many idiomatic expressions.
Spanish uses tener where English often uses "to be" (tener hambre, tener sueño).
Essential for everyday communication.
overview: Means "to have." It is one of the most frequently used verbs in Spanish and appears in numerous expressions describing age, needs, emotions, and obligations.

notes: Regular -ar verb.
Frequently used for drinking beverages.
Can also mean to take transportation or take action.
Common throughout the Spanish-speaking world.
overview: Means "to take," "to drink," or "to consume." It is a common verb used in daily life for actions involving food, beverages, transportation, and choices.

notes: Irregular verb.
Often contrasted with llevar ("to take/carry away").
Common in requests and everyday interactions.
Important distinction: traer = bring here; llevar = take there.

examples.en: He brought a gift for everyone.
I am going to bring my books.

overview: Means "to bring." It refers to carrying or moving something toward the speaker or a destination.

notes: Irregular verb.
Opposite of ir in many contexts.
Frequently used with places, events, and invitations.
Common expression: venir de ("to come from").
overview: Means "to come." It describes movement toward the speaker or another reference point and is one of the most common motion verbs in Spanish.

notes: Mostly regular but has some irregular forms.
Can mean both "to see" and "to watch."
Often contrasted with mirar, which emphasizes actively looking.
Extremely common in everyday speech.

examples.en: We watched a movie last night.

overview: Means "to see" or "to watch." It is one of the most frequently used perception verbs and is essential for discussing observation, entertainment, and experiences.

notes: Regular -ir verb.
Commonly used with locations.
Can refer to both physical residence and life experiences.
Helpful as a model verb for -ir conjugations.

examples.en: They lived there for ten years.
She wants to live an adventure.

overview: Means "to live." It is used to discuss residence, lifestyle, and existence. It also serves as a model for many regular -ir verbs.

notes: Stem-changing verb (o → ue) in many present forms.
Volver a + infinitive = "to do something again."
Commonly used for travel and repeated actions.
One of the most useful motion verbs.

examples.es: Vuelvo a casa después del trabajo.

examples.en: I am going to try it again.

overview: Means "to return," "to come back," or "to do again." It is frequently used for returning to places and, combined with a + infinitive, for describing repeated actions.
